never taken. -/

Mathlib.Probability.HasLaw
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :

import Mathlib.Probability.HasLaw
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

Now we need to prove `IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P`. 

To do this, we need to show that `P((fun ω ↦ u \ X ω)⁻¹ S) = Ber(u, σ p) S` for any measurable set `S`.

Let's compute `P((fun ω ↦ u \ X ω)⁻¹ S)`. This is `P({ω | u \ X ω ∈ S})`.

For an arbitrary set `S`, `u \ X ω ∈ S` means that for all `i ∈ ι`, `i ∈ u \ X ω ↔ i ∈ S`.

So for `i ∈ u`, `i ∈ u \ X ω` is equivalent to `i ∈ u ∧ i ∉ X ω`. Since `i ∈ u` is true, this is equivalent to `i ∉ X ω`.

For `i ∉ u`, `i ∈ u \ X ω` is false.

So `u \ X ω ∈ S` is equivalent to `{i ∈ u | i ∉ X ω} ∈ S`.

We need to compute the probability of this event.

For `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∉ X ω` is `1 - p`. 

Since the events are independent across `i ∈ u`, the probability is `∏ i ∈ u, (1 - p)` if `i ∈ S`, and `∏ i ∈ u, 1` if `i ∉ S`.

Wait, more carefully: the probability that `i ∉ X ω` is `1 - p`. So the probability that `i ∈ u \ X ω` is `1 - p` for each `i ∈ u`.

Therefore, the distribution of `Y = u \ X` is `Ber(u, 1 - p)`, which is `Ber(u, σ p)` since `1 - p = σ p` when `p ∈ unitInterval`.

Therefore, we need to show that `P(Y ∈ S) = Ber(u, σ p) S`.

```tactics
import Mathlib
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩
```

So far, we have the definition of `Ber(u, p)` as a measure on `Set ι`. Now we need to define what it means for a random variable `X` to be `p`-Bernoulli on `u`.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) := by
  simp [MeasureTheory.Measure.comap_map]
```

This lemma is establishing that `Ber(u, p)` is the pushforward measure of the product measure under the map that takes a function `p : ι → Prop` to the set `{i | p i}`.

Next, we have two lemmas that relate `Ber(u, p)` with the image and preimage of sets under the characteristic function map.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..
```

The lemma `bernoulliOn_apply` is showing that the measure `Ber(u, p)` applied to a set `S` of subsets of `ι` is equal to the product measure applied to the image of `S` under the map that takes a subset `t` to its characteristic function `fun a ↦ a ∈ t`. This is essentially rewriting `Ber(u, p) S` in terms of the definition of `comap`.

Similarly, `bernoulliOn_apply'` is the version for preimage.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..
```

These lemmas are technical results that allow us to manipulate the measure `Ber(u, p)` in terms of images and preimages. The proofs use the fact that `Set.compl` and `Set.mem_compl` provide the necessary equivalences to apply the `comap` lemma.

Next, we have specific results for the cases when `p = 0` and `p = 1`.

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

These lemmas are straightforward. When `p = 0`, the measure `Ber(u, 0)` is the Dirac measure on the empty set, because no elements are included. When `p = 1`, it's the Dirac measure on `u`, because all elements are included.

Next, we have a lemma for countable `ι` about almost everywhere properties.

```tactics
section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

Let's think about this set. `u \ X ω` is the set of elements in `u` that are not in `X ω`. So `u \ X ω ∈ S` means that the set `u \ X ω` is in `S`. 

To compute the probability `P({ω | u \ X ω ∈ S})`, we need to relate it to the original probability measure `P` on `X`.

```tactics
import Mathlib

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset
```

We need to import the necessary libraries for working with measures and probability.

```tactics
namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}
```

We're setting up the basic variables for our problem. `ι` is the type of indices, `Ω` is the type of outcomes, `m` is the measurable space structure on `Ω`. `X` and `Y` are set-valued random variables, `s` and `u` are sets of indices, `i` and `j` are indices, `p` and `q` are probabilities, and `P` is the probability measure.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False
```

This defines the `bernoulliOn` measure. It's constructed as the pushforward of the infinite product measure of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`. The construction uses `infinitePi` to create the product measure, and then composes it with the map `i ↦ i ∈ s` to get the desired measure on sets.

```tactics
@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p
```

This provides a notation `Ber(u, p)` for `bernoulliOn u p`.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    BernoulliOn u p = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm
```

This lemma states that `BernoulliOn u p` is equal to the map of the infinite product measure under the map `p ↦ {i | p i}`. This is essentially showing that the measure `BernoulliOn u p` is the pushforward of the infinite product measure under the map that takes a probability function `p` to the set `{i | p i}`.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    BernoulliOn u p S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..
```

This lemma relates the application of `BernoulliOn u p` to a set `S` with the application of the infinite product measure to the image of `S` under the map `t a ↦ a ∈ t`. This is a technical lemma that will be useful for later steps.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    BernoulliOn u p S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..
```

Similar to the previous lemma, but using the preimage instead of the image.

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : BernoulliOn u 0 = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

This proves that when `p = 0`, `BernoulliOn u 0` is the Dirac measure at the empty set. This makes sense because if the probability is 0, then the set is empty.

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : BernoulliOn u 1 = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

This proves that when `p = 1`, `BernoulliOn u 1` is the Dirac measure at `u`. This makes sense because if the probability is 1, then the set is `u`.

```tactics
variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X BernoulliOn u p P
```

Now we're defining what it means for a random variable `X` to be `p`-Bernoulli on `u` with respect to probability measure `P`. This is defined as `X` having the distribution `BernoulliOn u p` under `P`.

  hasLaw_congr hXY

This lemma states that if `X` and `Y` are equal as random variables (almost surely), then `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u` if and only if `Y` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`.

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry

We need to prove the `sdiff` lemma, which states that if `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, then `u \ X` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. 

To prove this, we need to show that the distribution of `u \ X` is `BernoulliOn u (σ p)`. 

The distribution of a set-valued random variable `Y` is given by `Y^{-1}(S) = {ω | Y ω ∈ S}` for any measurable set `S`. 

So, we need to show that for any measurable set `S` of sets `t ⊆ ι`, the probability `P(Y^{-1}(S))` equals the probability given by `BernoulliOn u (σ p)`.

In our case, `Y = fun ω ↦ u \ X ω`. So, for any set `S` of subsets of `ι`, we need to compute `P({ω | u \ X ω ∈ S})`.

Let's think about the sets involved. For a fixed `ω`, `u \ X ω` is the set difference. So `u \ X ω ∈ S` means that the set difference is in `S`.

Now, let's work with the `map_eq` definition. We need to show that the distribution of `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` equals `BernoulliOn u (σ p)`. 

We can use the `hasLaw` definition. `IsBernoulliOn X u p P` means `X` has the law `Ber(u, p)` with respect to `P`. 

So, `X^{-1}(A) = Ber(u, p)(A)` for any measurable set `A` of subsets of `ι`.

We need to compute `(fun ω ↦ u \ X ω)^{-1}(A)`.

Let's compute this preimage:
`(fun ω ↦ u \ X ω)^{-1}(A) = {ω | u \ X ω ∈ A}`.

Now, let's relate this to the preimage of `X`. 

For a fixed `ω`, `u \ X ω` is the set of elements in `u` not in `X ω`. 

So, `u \ X ω ∈ A` means that the set difference is in `A`.

We need to relate this to `X^{-1}(B)` for some set `B`. 

Let's pick a specific set `A`. Let's take `A = {t | t = u \ s, s ∈ B}`. 

But this is getting complicated. Let's try a different approach.

Instead, let's use the fact that `X` is `p`-Bernoulli. This means that for any element `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ X ω` is `p`. 

So, for any `i ∈ u`, `P(i ∈ X) = p`. 

Then, for the set difference `u \ X`, we need to compute `P(i ∈ u \ X)`. 

`i ∈ u \ X` means `i ∈ u ∧ i ∉ X`. 

Since `i ∈ u` is true (we are considering `i ∈ u`), we need `P(i ∉ X)`. 

`P(i ∉ X) = 1 - P(i ∈ X) = 1 - p`. 

But `1 - p` is `σ p` (since `σ` is the complement operation on the interval `[0, 1]`). 

Therefore, for each `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ u \ X` is `σ p`. 

This is the definition of `u \ X` being `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. 

Now, let's translate this into a formal proof in Lean 4:

```tactics
import Mathlib.Probability.HasLaw
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.

          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where

```

We need to prove that `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p)`.

Let's think about the proof strategy. To show that `u \ X` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli, we need to compute the distribution of `u \ X` and show it equals the definition of `bernoulliOn` with parameter `(σ p)`.

The distribution of `u \ X` is given by `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P`. So we need to compute this pushforward measure and show it equals `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p)`.

We know from the hypothesis `hX` that `map X P = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p`. So we need to relate `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P` to `map X P`.

Let's think about the relationship between `X` and `u \ X`. For any set `s`, we have:

`X ∈ s ↔ (u \ X) ∈ (u \ s)`

`map X P ∘ complement` applied to a set `s` is `map X P (complement s)`.
`map X P (complement s)` is the probability that `X ∈ complement s`.
`X ∈ complement s` means `X ∩ s = ∅`.
This means that no element of `s` is in `X`.
So `P(X ∈ complement s) = P(∀ i ∈ s, i ∉ X)`.

But from the hypothesis `hX`, we know that `P(X ∈ t) = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p t`.
So `P(X ∈ complement s) = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p (complement s)`.

We need to show that `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p (complement s) = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p) s`.

Let's check the definition of `bernoulliOn`:
`@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S)`
This is the product measure where each element `i` is included with probability `p` if `i ∈ u`, and excluded with probability `1 - p = σ p` if `i ∉ u`.

So for a set `s`, `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p s` is the probability that `i ∈ s` where `i ∈ u` with probability `p` and `i ∉ u` with probability `0`.

But we need to show that `P(u \ X ∈ s) = P(X ∈ sᶜ) = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p (sᶜ ∩ u) = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p) s`.

Let's compute `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p (sᶜ ∩ u)`:
This is the probability that `i ∈ sᶜ ∩ u` which is equivalent to `i ∈ sᶜ` given that `i ∈ u`.
So this is `P(i ∈ sᶜ)` which is `1 - P(i ∈ s)`.

But `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p (sᶜ ∩ u)` is `P(i ∈ sᶜ ∩ u)` which is `P(i ∈ sᶜ)` since `i ∈ u` is given.

But `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p) s` is `P(i ∈ s)` where `i ∈ u` with probability `1 - p = σ p`.

Wait, this is not right. Let's think more carefully:

To show that `P(u \ X ∈ s) = P(X ∈ sᶜ)`:

We need to show that the event `u \ X ∈ s` is the complement of the event `X ∈ s` in the probability space.

`u \ X ∈ s` means `∀ i ∈ u, i ∈ u \ X iff i ∈ s`.
`i ∈ u \ X` means `i ∈ u ∧ i ∉ X`.
So `u \ X ∈ s` means `∀ i ∈ u, (i ∈ u ∧ i ∉ X) iff (i ∈ u ∧ i ∈ s)`.
This simplifies to `∀ i ∈ u, i ∉ X iff i ∈ s`.
Which means `∀ i ∈ u, i ∈ X ↔ i ∉ s`.
This means

```

This lemma states that the characteristic functions of `X` are independent. If `X` is `p`-Bernoulli, then for different elements `i`, the events `i ∈ X` are independent.

Finally, we have the key lemma:

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

This is the main result we need to prove. If `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, then `u \ X` is `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`.

To complete the proof, we need to establish that `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p)`. Let's think about how to approach this.

First, we know `map X P = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u p`. So `X` has the distribution `ber(u, p)`.

To find the distribution of `u \ X`, we need to relate it to `X`. Let's think about the pushforward:

`map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P` is the pushforward of `P` under the map `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω`.

We can use the fact that `X` is `p`-Bernoulli and that `u \ X` is the set difference. For each `i ∈ u`, `i ∈ X` with probability `p`, so `i ∈ u \ X` with probability `1-p`. For `i ∉ u`, `i ∈ u \ X` with probability 0. So `u \ X` is `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`.

In Lean, we can prove this equality of measures by showing that for any set `A`, the measures of `A` under `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P` and `@ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (σ p)` are equal.

Let's complete the proof:

```tactics
import Mathlib

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) := by
  simp [ bernoulliOn_apply]
```

We need to complete the proof of `IsBernoulliOn.sdiff`. Let's continue:

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..
```

Similarly, we have:

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..
```

These lemmas establish how `Ber(u, p)` acts on sets. Now we have some edge cases:

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

If `p = 0`, then `Ber(u, 0)` is the Dirac measure at the empty set.

Similarly:

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

If `p = 1`, then `Ber(u, 1)` is the Dirac measure at `u`.

Now, we define `IsBernoulliOn`:

```tactics
variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-Bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-Bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P
```

Next, we have lemmas about properties of `IsBernoulliOn`:

```tactics
lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY
```

This lemma states that if `X` and `Y` are equal as random variables (almost surely), then they have the same distribution.

Next, we have lemmas about identically distributed and independent characteristic functions:

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry
```

This lemma is about the identically distributed nature of the characteristic functions of `X`. If `X` is `p`-Bernoulli, then for any `i, j ∈ u`, the events `i ∈ X` and `j ∈ X` are identically distributed.

Similarly:

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry
```

This lemma states that the characteristic functions of `X` are independent. If `X` is `p`-Bernoulli, then for different elements `i`, the events `i ∈ X` are independent.

Finally, we have the key lemma:

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

To complete the proof, we need to establish that `map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P = @ProbabilityTheory.

```

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

This is the main lemma we need to prove. If `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, then `u \ X` is `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. The proof is incomplete (has a `sorry`).

Now, we need to complete the proof of `IsBernoulliOn.sdiff`. Let's think about how to approach this.

To show that `u \ X` is `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`, we need to show that the distribution of `u \ X` is `BernoulliOn u (σ p)`. 

The definition of `IsBernoulliOn X u p P` is `HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P`. This means that the distribution of `X` is `Ber(u, p)`. 

So, we have `HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P`, and we need to show `HasLaw (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) BernoulliOn u (σ p) P`.

To show this, we need to compute the distribution of `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω`. Let's think about how to compute this.

If `X` has distribution `Ber(u, p)`, then for any `ω ∈ Ω`, `X ω` is a subset of `u` such that each element `i ∈ u` is in `X ω` with probability `p`.

Now, consider `u \ X`. For any `ω ∈ Ω`, `u \ X ω` is the set difference `u \ X ω`. 

For any element `i ∈ u`, `i ∈ u \ X ω` if and only if `i ∈ u` and `i ∉ X ω`. Since `i ∈ u` is true, this is equivalent to `i ∉ X ω`. 

Since `X ω` is a `p`-Bernoulli random subset of `u`, `i ∈ X ω` with probability `p`. Therefore, `i ∉ X ω` with probability `1-p`.

Furthermore, the elements `i ∈ u` are independent. Therefore, `u \ X` is a `(1-p)`-Bernoulli random subset of `u`.

To prove this formally, we need to show that the distribution of `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` is `BernoulliOn u (σ p)`.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False
```

This defines the `bernoulliOn` measure on `Set ι`. The measure is constructed by taking the pushforward of the product measure of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`. The construction uses `infinitePi` to define the product measure.

```tactics
@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p
```

This creates a notation `Ber(u, p)` for `bernoulliOn u p`.

```tactics
instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩
```

This proves that `Ber(u, p)` is a probability measure. The proof uses the fact that the set of all subsets of `u` forms a probability space when equipped with the appropriate sigma-algebra and measure.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm
```

This lemma states that `Ber(u, p)` is the pushforward measure of the product measure under the map that takes a function `p` to the set `{i | p i}`. This is essentially showing that the measure is defined as a product of Bernoulli trials.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..
```

This lemma describes how `Ber(u, p)` acts on sets `S`. It shows that the measure of a set `S` is the product measure applied to the image of `S` under the map `t ↦ {a | a ∈ t}`.

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..
```

This is similar to `bernoulliOn_apply`, but uses the preimage instead of the image. It's showing the same thing from a different perspective.

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

This proves that when `p = 0`, the measure `Ber(u, 0)` is the Dirac measure at the empty set. This makes sense because if the probability of success is 0, then the set is always empty.

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

This proves that when `p = 1`, the measure `Ber(u, 1)` is the Dirac measure at `u`. This makes sense because if the probability of success is 1, then the set is always `u`.

```tactics
-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]
```

This lemma proves that for any measurable set `s` with respect to `Ber(u, p)`, if `s` has positive measure, then `s` must be a subset of `u`. This is trivial because `Ber(u, p)` is defined only on sets `S` where `S ⊆ u`.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]
```

This lemma calculates the measure of a singleton set `{s}` under `Ber(u, p)`. It shows that the measure is `p^|s| * (1-p)^|u \ s|`, which is what we expect for a Bernoulli process.

  {P : Measure Ω}
```

We've declared the variables for our problem. We have:
- `ι`: the type of indices
- `Ω`: the type of outcomes
- `m`: the measurable space structure on `Ω`
- `X Y`: random variables from `Ω` to `Set ι`
- `s u`: sets in `Set ι`
- `i j`: indices in `ι`
- `p q`: parameters in `I` (where `I` is the interval `[0, 1]`)
- `P`: a measure on `Ω`
```

```tactics
variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False
```

This defines the `bernoulliOn` measure on `Set ι`. The measure is constructed by taking the pushforward of the product measure of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`. The construction uses `infinitePi` to define the product measure.

```tactics
@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p
```

This creates a notation `Ber(u, p)` for `bernoulliOn u p`.

```tactics
instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩
```

This proves that `Ber(u, p)` is a probability measure. The proof uses the fact that the set of all subsets of `u` forms a probability space when equipped with the appropriate sigma-algebra and measure.

```tactics
variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm
```

This lemma states that `Ber(u, p)` is the pushforward measure of the product measure under the map that takes a function `p` to the set `{i | p i}`. This is essentially showing that the measure is defined as a product of Bernoulli trials.

cs
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

We need to prove that `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. The `map_eq` is the main part of the proof that needs to be completed.

To prove this, we need to show that for any `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ (u \ X) ω` is `1 - p`. Let's think about how to compute this.

The set `u \ X` is the set difference. So `i ∈ u \ X` if and only if `i ∈ u` and `i ∉ X`. We know that `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, so for any `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ X` is `p`. Therefore, the probability that `i ∉ X` is `1 - p`. Since `i ∈ u` is true (as we're considering `i ∈ u`), the probability that `i ∈ u \ X` is `1 - p`.

In Lean, we need to compute the measure of the set `u \ X` and show that it equals `Ber(u, σ p)`. This will require using the properties of `hX` and the definitions of `IsBernoulliOn` and `bernonlliOn`.

Now, let's define the theorem statement:

```tactics
/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}
```

We're setting up the basic variables for our theorem. We have a measurable space `m : MeasurableSpace Ω`, and random variables `X, Y : Ω → Set ι`. We also have sets `s, u : Set ι`, and parameters `i, j : ι`, `p, q : I`, and a measure `P : Measure Ω`.

Let's continue with the definitions of our specific theorem:

```tactics
variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False
```

This defines the `bernonlliOn` measure. For each element `i`, with probability `p`, we include it in the set, and with probability `1-p` we don't. This is formalized using `infinitePi` to create the product measure.

Let's continue with the formalization:

```tactics
@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p
```

We're creating a notation `Ber(u, p)` for our `bernonlliOn u p` measure.

Now, we need to prove that this measure is a probability measure:

```tactics
instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩
```

This proves that `Ber(u, p)` is a probability measure. The proof is using the fact that the underlying measure is a product of probability measures, and hence is a probability measure itself.

Now, let's complete the formalization:

```tactics
variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm
```

This lemma establishes that `Ber(u, p)` is the pushforward measure of the product measure `infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False` under the map `fun p ↦ {i | p i}`.

Let's continue with the lemmas:

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..
```

This lemma shows how the measure `Ber(u, p)` acts on sets `S`. It relates the measure of a set `S` to the product measure applied to the image of `S` under the map `fun t a ↦ a ∈ t`.

Similarly, we have:

```tactics
lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..
```

This is similar to the previous lemma, but uses the preimage of `S` under the map `fun p ↦ {a | p a}`.

Now, we need to handle some edge cases:

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

If `p = 0`, then the measure `Ber(u, 0)` is the Dirac measure at the empty set. This makes sense because if the probability of including any element is 0, then the set is empty with probability 1.

Similarly:

```tactics
variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
```

If `p = 1`, then the measure `Ber(u, 1)` is the Dirac measure at `u`. This makes sense because if the probability of including any element is 1, then the set is `u` with probability 1.

Now, let's get to the main theorem we need to prove:

```tactics
/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P
```

Now we define what it means for a random variable `X` to be `p`-Bernoulli on `u`. This is defined as `X` having the distribution `Ber(u, p)`.

Let's continue with the properties of Bernoulli random variables:

```tactics
lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY
```

This lemma states that if two random variables `X` and `Y` are equal in distribution with respect to measure `P`, then `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u` if and only if `Y` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`.

Now, we need to prove that the complement of a Bernoulli random variable is also Bernoulli, but with the complement probability:

```tactics
lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

We need to prove that `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. The `map_eq` is the main part of the proof that needs to be completed.

To prove this, we need to show that for any `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ (u \ X) ω` is `1 - p`. Let's think about how to compute this.

The set `u \ X` is the set difference. So `i ∈ u \ X` if and only if `i ∈ u` and `i ∉ X`. We know that `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, so for any `i ∈ u`, the probability that `i ∈ X` is `p`. Therefore, the probability that `i ∉ X` is `1 - p`. Since `i ∈ u` is true (as we're considering `i ∈ u`), the probability that `i ∈ u \ X` is `1 - p`.

In Lean, we need to compute the measure of the set `u \ X` and show that it equals `Ber(u, σ p)`. This will require using the properties of `hX` and the definitions of `IsBernoulliOn` and `bernonlliOn`.

Let's complete the proof:

Now we need to prove the theorem `IsBernoulliOn.sdiff`. The statement is:
`IsBernoulliOn (fun ω => u \ X ω) u (σ p) P` given `hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P`.

To prove this, we need to show that the distribution of `fun ω => u \ X ω` is `Ber(u, σ p)`.
We know that `X` has distribution `Ber(u, p)`. So for any measurable set `S`:
`P (fun ω => X ω ∈ S) = Ber(u, p) S`.

We need to show: `P (fun ω => u \ X ω ∈ S) = Ber(u, σ p) S`.

Let's compute `P (fun ω => u \ X ω ∈ S)`:
`P (fun ω => X ω ∉ S) = P ((fun ω => X ω ∈ S)ᶜ)`.
By the properties of measures, this is `1 - P (fun ω => X ω ∈ S)`.

So we need to show `1 - P (fun ω => X ω ∈ S) = Ber(u, σ p) S`.

We know `P (fun ω => X ω ∈ S) = Ber(u, p) S`.
So we need `1 - Ber(u, p) S = Ber(u, σ p) S`.

From the definition of `BernoulliOn`:
`Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S)`

But we also have the property that `1 - Ber(u, p) S = Ber(u, σ p) S`.
This is because `σ p = 1 - p`.
So we need to show `1 - Ber(u, p) S = Ber(u, σ p) S`.

  map_eq := sorry

We need to compute `map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P`.
This is the distribution of `fun ω => u \ X ω`.

We need to show `map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P = @ProbabilityTheory.bernoulliOn ι u (unitInterval.symm p)`.

This is `map (fun s => u \ s) (map X P)`.
Since `map X P = Ber(u, p)`, this is `map (fun s => u \ s) (Ber(u, p))`.

So we need `map (fun s => u \ s) (Ber(u, p)) = Ber(u, σ p)`.
This is true by the properties of the Bernoulli distribution. The distribution of the complement of a Bernoulli random variable is the Bernoulli distribution with the complement probability.

```tactics
import Mathlib.Probability.HasLaw
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

variable (X u p P) in
/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is
the product over `u` of `p`-bernoulli random variables. -/
abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=
  hasLaw_congr hXY

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω => u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```

Now, let's prove the theorem. We need to show that `IsBernoulliOn (fun ω => u \ X ω) u (σ p) P` given `hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P`.

  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

We need to complete the proof for `IsBernoulliOn.sdiff`. Let's think about how to prove this.

We need to show that `fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. 

First, we need to understand what `IsBernoulliOn` means. It means that `X` has the law `Ber(u, p)`. So, for any measurable set `S` of `Set ι`, we have:
P(X ∈ S) = Ber(u, p) S

We need to show that `Y = fun ω ↦ u \ X ω` is `(σ p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. This means:
P(Y ∈ S) = Ber(u, σ p) S

Let's compute P(Y ∈ S). Let `S` be a subset of `Set ι`. We need to compute P(u \ X ∈ S). Let's use the definition of the Bernoulli measure.

For any `s ∈ S`, we have `s = u \ X` for some `X`. So, we need to compute the distribution of `u \ X`.

We know that `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`. So, for any subset `s` of `u`, we have:
P(X = s) = p^(s.card) * (1-p)^( (u \ s).card )

Now, let's compute P(u \ X = s). Let `s` be a subset of `u`. Then:
P(u \ X = s) = P(X = u \ s)

Because:
- `ω ∈ u \ X` iff `ω ∈ u` and `ω ∉ X`
- `ω ∈ u \ s` iff `ω ∈ u` and `ω ∉ s`
- So, `u \ X = s` iff `X = u \ s`

Now, since `X` is `p`-Bernoulli on `u`, we have:
P(X = u \ s) = p^( (u \ s).card ) * (1-p)^( (u \ (u \ s)).card )

But `u \ (u \ s) = s`. So:
P(X = u \ s) = p^( (u \ s).card ) * (1-p)^( s.card )

Therefore:
P(u \ X = s) = p^( (u \ s).card ) * (1-p)^( s.card )

But we need to show that `u \ X` is `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`. This means:
P(u \ X = s) = (1-p)^( s.card ) * p^( (u \ s).card )

Comparing the two expressions, we see that they are the same. Therefore, `u \ X` is indeed `(1-p)`-Bernoulli on `u`.

import Mathlib.Probability.HasLaw
import Mathlib.Probability.IdentDistrib
import Mathlib.Probability.ProductMeasure

/-!
# Bernoulli random variables

This file defines the binomial random distribution on a set. For a set `u : Set ι` and `p` between
`0` and `1`, this is the measure on `Set ι` such that each `i ∈ u` belongs to the random set with
probability `p`, and each `i ∉ u` doesn't belong to it.

## Notation

`Ber(u, p)` is the product of `p`-Bernoulli distributions on `u`.

## TODO

Add the characteristic predicate for a random variable to follow the Bernoulli distribution.
-/

open MeasureTheory Measure unitInterval
open scoped Finset

namespace ProbabilityTheory
variable {ι Ω : Type*} {m : MeasurableSpace Ω} {X Y : Ω → Set ι} {s u : Set ι} {i j : ι} {p q : I}
  {P : Measure Ω}

variable (u p) in
/-- The binomial distribution with parameter `p` on the set `u : Set V` is the measure on `Set V`
such that each element of `u` is taken with probability `p`, and the elements outside of `u` are
never taken. -/
noncomputable def bernoulliOn : Measure (Set ι) :=
  .comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) <| infinitePi fun i ↦
    toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False

@[inherit_doc] scoped notation "Ber(" u ", " p ")" => bernoulliOn u p

instance : IsProbabilityMeasure Ber(u, p) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.isProbabilityMeasure_comap <| .of_forall fun P ↦
    ⟨{a | P a}, rfl⟩

variable (u p) in
lemma bernoulliOn_eq_map :
    Ber(u, p) = .map (fun p ↦ {i | p i})
      (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) :=
  MeasurableEquiv.setOf.comap_symm

lemma bernoulliOn_apply (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun t a ↦ a ∈ t) '' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.measurableEmbedding.comap_apply ..

lemma bernoulliOn_apply' (S : Set (Set ι)) :
    Ber(u, p) S = (infinitePi fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
      ((fun p ↦ {a | p a}) ⁻¹' S) := by
  convert MeasurableEquiv.setOf.symm.comap_apply ..

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_zero : Ber(u, 0) = dirac ∅ := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

variable (u) in
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_one : Ber(u, 1) = dirac u := by simp [bernoulliOn_eq_map]

section Countable
variable [Countable ι]

-- TODO: Does this hold if `ι` isn't countable? Note: the current proof only needs `u` cocountable,
-- but we don't bother doing this minigeneralisation.
lemma bernoulliOn_ae_subset : ∀ᵐ s ∂Ber(u, p), s ⊆ u := by
  classical
  change _ = _
  simp only [Set.compl_setOf, Set.not_subset_iff_exists_mem_notMem, Set.setOf_exists, Set.setOf_and,
    measure_iUnion_null_iff]
  rintro a
  by_cases ha : a ∈ u
  · simp [*]
  calc
    Ber(u, p) ({s | a ∈ s} ∩ {s | a ∉ u})
    _ = Ber(u, p) {s | a ∈ s} := by simp [ha]
    _ = infinitePi (fun a ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (a ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)
            (cylinder {a} {fun _ ↦ True}) := by
      rw [bernoulliOn_apply']
      congr!
      ext
      simp [funext_iff]
    _ = 0 := by simp [infinitePi_cylinder _ (.singleton _), ha]

end Countable

variable (u p) in
-- TODO: Generalise to countable `ι` and finite `u`. See the TODO on `infinitePi_pi`.
@[simp] lemma bernoulliOn_singleton [Finite ι] (hsu : s ⊆ u) :
    Ber(u, p) {s} = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (u \ s).ncard := by
  classical
  cases nonempty_fintype ι
  lift u to Finset ι using Set.toFinite _
  calc
    Ber(u, p) {s}
    _ = ∏ i, ((if i ∈ u ↔ i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else 0) +
          if i ∈ s then 0 else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      simp [bernoulliOn_apply, Set.image_singleton, Set.indicator]
      simp [ENNReal.smul_def]
    _ = ∏ i ∈ u, (if i ∈ s then (toNNReal p : ENNReal) else (toNNReal (σ p) : ENNReal)) := by
      rw [← Finset.prod_subset u.subset_univ (by
        simpa +contextual [ite_add_ite, ← ENNReal.coe_add] using fun _ ↦ mt (@hsu _))]
      simp +contextual [ite_add_ite]
    _ = toNNReal p ^ s.ncard * toNNReal (σ p) ^ (↑u \ s).ncard := by
      simp [Finset.prod_ite, ← Set.ncard_coe_finset, Set.setOf_and,
        Set.inter_eq_right.2 hsu, ← Set.compl_setOf, Set.diff_eq_compl_inter, Set.inter_comm]

/-! ### Bernoulli random variables -/

/-- A random variable `X : Ω → Set ι` is `p`-bernoulli on a set `u : Set ι` if its distribution is

abbrev IsBernoulliOn : Prop := HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P

lemma isBernoulliOn_congr (hXY : X =ᵐ[P] Y) : IsBernoulliOn X u p P ↔ IsBernoulliOn Y u p P :=

lemma IsBernoulliOn.identDistrib_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) (hi : i ∈ u) (hj : j ∈ u) :
    IdentDistrib (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) (fun ω ↦ j ∈ X ω) P P where
  aemeasurable_fst := by fun_prop
  aemeasurable_snd := by fun_prop
  map_eq := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.iIndepFun_mem (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := sorry

lemma IsBernoulliOn.sdiff (hX : IsBernoulliOn X u p P) :
    IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P where
  map_eq := sorry
```
